title: Solomon asks for wisdom at Gibeon
summary_text: Solomon offers sacrifice at Gibeon and asks God for wisdom rather than riches or long life. God grants him wisdom, knowledge, wealth, and honor beyond any king before or after him. The chapter establishes that Solomon's greatness begins with dependence on God, not self-sufficiency.
key_points: Solomon worships at Gibeon.
He asks for wisdom to govern well.
God grants him wealth and honor too.
The chapter sets the tone for his reign.

title: Preparing materials for the temple
summary_text: Solomon arranges for cedar, skilled labor, and abundant materials so the temple can be built with excellence. He writes to Hiram and explains that the house will be for the great God whose name fills heaven and earth. The chapter shows deliberate preparation before construction begins.
key_points: Solomon seeks expert workers and materials.
Hiram provides cedar and craftsmanship.
The temple is described as a house for God.
Preparation is detailed before building starts.
evidence_phrases: the house for the name of the LORD

title: The temple is built on Mount Moriah
summary_text: Solomon begins construction of the temple on Mount Moriah, the place associated with Abraham's sacrifice. The chapter carefully describes the dimensions, materials, and ornamentation of the house. The emphasis falls on holiness, continuity, and the visible beauty of worship.
key_points: The temple is built on Mount Moriah.
The inner sanctuary receives special attention.
The decorations emphasize holiness and beauty.
The chapter ties the site to covenant memory.

title: Temple furnishings and bronze work
summary_text: The chapter details the bronze and gold furnishings that equip the temple for worship. Hiram's craftsmanship produces the great sea, the pillars, the stands, and the utensils used in service. The list underscores that sacred space is furnished carefully for holy use.
key_points: The bronze sea and pillars are described.
Lavish utensils are made for worship.
The temple is fitted for priestly service.
The chapter catalogs the sacred objects.

title: The ark enters the completed temple
summary_text: The ark is brought into the temple with priests, Levites, singers, and sacrifice. When the ark is settled in the most holy place, the glory of the LORD fills the house. The chapter turns the building project into a living sanctuary where God's presence is openly recognized.
key_points: The ark is carried into the temple.
Worshipers bring sacrifice and song.
The house is filled with God's glory.
The temple becomes a place of manifest presence.

title: Solomon's dedication prayer
summary_text: Solomon blesses the people and prays that God will hear from heaven when Israel turns toward this house. He asks for justice, forgiveness, mercy, and covenant faithfulness in every kind of distress. The prayer makes the temple a place where repentance and God's response belong together.
key_points: Solomon blesses the assembled people.
He prays for many kinds of distress.
He asks God to hear and forgive.
The temple is framed as a house of prayer.
evidence_phrases: when they pray toward this place

title: Fire, glory, and covenant blessing
summary_text: Fire falls from heaven to consume the sacrifice, and the glory of the LORD fills the house again. God answers Solomon's prayer by accepting the temple and then warning that obedience must continue if blessing is to remain. The chapter combines divine approval with a serious call to covenant faithfulness.
key_points: Fire comes down from heaven.
The temple is filled with glory.
The LORD confirms his acceptance.
Blessing depends on continued obedience.

title: Solomon's building works and trade
summary_text: Solomon expands his building program with cities, fortresses, and infrastructure, while also governing labor and trade. The chapter records his organization of the people and his cooperation with Hiram on sea traffic and gold. It presents a strong kingdom built on planning, labor, and international reach.
key_points: Solomon builds cities and fortifications.
Labor is organized across the kingdom.
Trade extends Israel's reach overseas.
The reign is marked by planning and abundance.

title: The queen of Sheba and Solomon's splendor
summary_text: The queen of Sheba comes to test Solomon and leaves amazed by his wisdom, wealth, and the order of his house. She blesses the LORD who set him on the throne and recognizes that his kingdom displays divine favor. The chapter then summarizes Solomon's unmatched splendor and closes his reign with honor.
key_points: The queen tests Solomon with hard questions.
She praises the LORD for Solomon's throne.
The kingdom's wealth is described in detail.
Solomon's reign is summarized as remarkable.
evidence_phrases: there was no more spirit in her
all the kings of the earth

title: Rehoboam and the kingdom's division
summary_text: Rehoboam rejects the people's appeal for relief and answers with harsher threats than Solomon had used. The northern tribes break away under Jeroboam, leaving Judah and Israel divided. The chapter marks the loss of unity as a consequence of proud leadership.
key_points: Rehoboam refuses the elders' advice.
The northern tribes reject David's house.
Jeroboam becomes king over Israel.
The kingdom is split by harsh words.
evidence_phrases: my father made your yoke heavy
what portion have we in David

title: Rehoboam strengthens Judah
summary_text: Rehoboam plans to fight the breakaway tribes, but God forbids the civil war and preserves the division. Judah is strengthened with fortified cities, and priests and Levites from the north come south to support the true temple. The chapter shows that God still cares for David's line even after the rupture.
key_points: God prevents a war between brothers.
Rehoboam fortifies Judah's cities.
Priests and Levites leave the north for Jerusalem.
The kingdom of Judah is strengthened.
evidence_phrases: walked in the ways of David

title: Shishak invades and Rehoboam humbles himself
summary_text: After Judah turns away from the LORD, Shishak of Egypt invades and strips the kingdom of its wealth. Rehoboam and the leaders humble themselves, so destruction is limited rather than total. The chapter makes clear that repentance can soften judgment even when the damage is real.
key_points: Judah abandons the LORD's law.
Shishak invades Jerusalem.
Rehoboam and the princes humble themselves.
Judgment is moderated, though the kingdom suffers.
evidence_phrases: they forsook the law of the LORD
I have left you in his hand

title: Abijah confronts Jeroboam
summary_text: Abijah stands on Mount Zemaraim and declares that Judah still has the priesthood, the temple, and the covenant promise, while Jeroboam has fought against the LORD. He cries for help in battle and wins a surprising victory. The chapter highlights that God can preserve David's line even when Judah is weak.
key_points: Abijah defends the temple order.
Jeroboam is accused of fighting against God.
Judah cries out and wins the battle.
The LORD preserves David's house.

title: Asa seeks the LORD and defeats the invader
summary_text: Asa removes idols and seeks the LORD, and God gives Judah peace and then victory over a massive invading force. His prayer in the face of the Ethiopian army shows trust in divine help rather than military strength. The chapter presents Asa's early reign as a model of reform and dependence.
key_points: Asa removes idols from Judah.
He seeks the LORD during danger.
God grants a great victory.
Peace follows the battle.
evidence_phrases: did that which was good and right
they carried away very much spoil

title: Azariah warns Asa to keep seeking God
summary_text: The prophet Azariah reminds Asa and Judah that the LORD is with those who seek him and will leave those who forsake him. Asa responds by removing more idols and renewing the covenant with joyful sacrifice. The chapter links prophetic warning with practical reform and worship.
key_points: Azariah speaks of reward for seeking God.
Asa removes remaining idolatry.
The people renew the covenant.
Reform is backed by public sacrifice.
evidence_phrases: be strong and do not let your hands be weak

title: Asa relies on Aram and is rebuked
summary_text: When Baasha threatens Judah, Asa turns to Aram for help instead of relying on the LORD. Hanani rebukes the king for depending on human alliances, and Asa reacts with anger rather than repentance. The chapter marks the shift from early faithfulness to late compromise.
key_points: Asa allies with Aram against Baasha.
Hanani warns him that he relied on man, not God.
Asa responds with anger and oppression.
The end of his reign is marked by sickness.
evidence_phrases: you relied on the king of Syria
from now on you shall have wars
he was angry with the seer

title: Jehoshaphat teaches the law
summary_text: Jehoshaphat strengthens Judah by sending officials, priests, and Levites to teach the book of the law through the towns. The LORD establishes his kingdom and gives him tribute from surrounding nations. The chapter portrays a reign marked by instruction, stability, and respect from outside powers.
key_points: Teachers are sent through Judah.
The book of the law is taught publicly.
Surrounding nations bring tribute.
Jehoshaphat's kingdom grows strong.

title: Jehoshaphat joins Ahab and hears Micaiah
summary_text: Jehoshaphat forms an alliance with Ahab, but Micaiah alone speaks the true word that the battle will end in disaster. The false prophets promise victory, yet Ahab's attempt to disguise himself cannot avoid the judgment announced. The chapter warns against compromising alliances even when the language of religion sounds confident.
key_points: Jehoshaphat joins Ahab in battle.
Micaiah predicts defeat.
Ahab ignores the warning.
The disguise does not stop judgment.
evidence_phrases: Is there not a prophet of the LORD
a certain man drew a bow

title: Jehoshaphat appoints judges
summary_text: Jehoshaphat returns from battle and sets up judges, Levites, priests, and heads of families to administer justice in the land. He tells them to judge for the LORD and to act in the fear of God rather than for human gain. The chapter turns from military politics to covenant justice and accountability.
key_points: Judges are appointed throughout Judah.
The judges are told to act for the LORD.
Justice must be free from partiality.
Religious and civil leadership work together.

title: Moab and Ammon are defeated by prayer
summary_text: A vast enemy coalition comes against Judah, but Jehoshaphat responds by praying and calling the people to seek the LORD. God answers through Jahaziel and then turns the enemy armies against each other. The chapter emphasizes that praise and trust can defeat what military force cannot.
key_points: Jehoshaphat fasts and prays.
Jahaziel announces the LORD's deliverance.
Judah sings praise before the battle.
The enemy collapses without Judah's sword.
evidence_phrases: we do not know what to do

title: Jehoram's evil reign in Judah
summary_text: Jehoram kills his brothers, marries into Ahab's line, and leads Judah into deeper rebellion. Edom and Libnah revolt against him, and Elijah's letter announces disaster for his house. The chapter presents a king whose choices unravel both family and nation.
key_points: Jehoram kills his brothers to secure power.
Judah adopts Ahab-like idolatry.
Edom and Libnah revolt.
The king's sickness and death match the prophecy.
evidence_phrases: walked in the way of the kings of Israel

title: Ahaziah and Athaliah seize the moment
summary_text: Ahaziah follows the wicked counsel around him and is drawn into the same destructive orbit as Ahab's house. His death leaves Athaliah to seize control in Judah, and the chapter briefly records the instability of the throne. The story prepares the way for God's hidden preservation of David's line.
key_points: Ahaziah is influenced by Ahab's family.
He dies after a brief reign.
Athaliah takes power in Judah.
The throne is left vulnerable to God's intervention.

title: Jehoiada crowns Joash and removes Athaliah
summary_text: Jehoiada gathers the leaders, crowns Joash, and establishes a covenant between the people, the king, and the LORD. Athaliah is executed, and the temple worship is restored with guards, singers, and proper order. The chapter shows priestly faithfulness preserving David's line.
key_points: Jehoiada secretly gathers support for Joash.
Joash is crowned before the people.
Athaliah is removed from power.
Temple worship is restored after the coup.
evidence_phrases: make a covenant with the king

title: Joash repairs the temple, then kills Zechariah
summary_text: Joash begins by repairing the temple and restoring proper funding, but after Jehoiada dies he listens to corrupt advisers. He turns against the word of the LORD and murders Zechariah, bringing guilt on Judah. The chapter moves from early reform to tragic betrayal.
key_points: The temple is repaired with collected offerings.
Joash listens to leaders after Jehoiada dies.
Zechariah rebukes the people and is killed.
Joash's good beginning ends in judgment.
evidence_phrases: repair the house of the LORD

title: Amaziah's pride and defeat
summary_text: Amaziah begins well by obeying the prophet and defeating Edom, but pride later leads him to provoke Israel and suffer humiliation. He brings back foreign gods from Edom and refuses correction, setting up his downfall. The chapter warns that partial obedience cannot sustain a king who becomes self-confident.
key_points: Amaziah wins against Edom.
He ignores wise warning and attacks Israel.
His pride brings defeat and plunder.
The king's life ends in conspiracy.
evidence_phrases: why should you be struck down
your heart has lifted you up
you have sought after the gods
he was buried with his fathers

title: Uzziah's strength and downfall
summary_text: Uzziah prospers while he seeks the LORD, but pride leads him to intrude into priestly duties. When he burns incense in the temple, leprosy breaks out and he is removed from public life. The chapter contrasts blessing under humility with judgment under self-exaltation.
key_points: Uzziah is strengthened as long as he seeks God.
He grows proud in his success.
The priests resist his unlawful action.
Leprosy removes him from the temple and the throne.
evidence_phrases: as long as he sought the LORD
he dwelled in a separate house

title: Jotham rules with steady obedience
summary_text: Jotham builds, governs, and strengthens Judah without falling into his father's mistakes. He does not enter the temple unlawfully, and his reign is marked by steady military and civic work. The chapter is brief but clear: quiet faithfulness is better than dramatic pride.
key_points: Jotham builds and fortifies Judah.
He avoids the arrogance that ruined Uzziah.
The Ammonites pay tribute.
His reign is summarized as upright.
evidence_phrases: he did that which was right
the high gate of the house of the LORD
in the sight of the LORD

title: Ahaz falls into deep idolatry
summary_text: Ahaz turns Judah to Baal worship, child sacrifice, and foreign altars, and the kingdom collapses under repeated defeats. Aram and Israel punish Judah, and Ahaz responds by trusting Assyria even more. The chapter presents a king whose unbelief brings national humiliation.
key_points: Ahaz promotes pagan worship.
Judah suffers defeats at the hands of neighbors.
Assyria becomes his false protector.
The temple is further profaned.
evidence_phrases: made his sons pass through the fire
shut up the doors of the house

title: Hezekiah reopens and purifies the temple
summary_text: Hezekiah opens the doors of the house of the LORD and orders the priests and Levites to cleanse it. He recognizes that Judah's trouble came from abandoning worship and leads the nation back to sacrifice and song. The chapter begins the reform era by restoring the temple before anything else.
key_points: The temple doors are reopened.
The priests and Levites are sanctified for service.
Hezekiah confesses Judah's earlier guilt.
Worship is restored with sacrifice and song.

title: Hezekiah restores the Passover
summary_text: Hezekiah sends invitations throughout Israel and Judah so the people will return for Passover in Jerusalem. Some mock the call, but many humble themselves and come, and the feast is kept with unusual joy. The chapter shows that repentance can gather scattered worshipers back around God's table.
key_points: Letters invite all Israel to the feast.
Some mock, but many respond humbly.
The Passover is kept in Jerusalem.
The celebration is extended because the people rejoice.

title: Offerings, tithes, and orderly provision
summary_text: The people respond to the reform by bringing abundant offerings, tithes, and firstfruits for the priests and Levites. Hezekiah organizes the storehouses and appoints men to distribute the gifts faithfully. The chapter shows that revived worship also requires practical systems of provision.
key_points: The people bring abundant offerings.
Storehouses are prepared for the gifts.
Support is distributed to priests and Levites.
Hezekiah's reform includes practical administration.
evidence_phrases: the children of Israel brought in abundance
he did what was good and right

title: Sennacherib attacks and Hezekiah grows proud
summary_text: Sennacherib threatens Judah, but Hezekiah strengthens the city and seeks the LORD, who sends deliverance from the Assyrian army. Later Hezekiah becomes proud over the riches God gave him, and the chapter records both his faith and his failing. The mix of rescue and pride keeps the reform story realistic.
key_points: Hezekiah prepares Judah for siege.
God delivers Jerusalem from Assyria.
Hezekiah recovers from illness and sickness.
Pride enters after victory and healing.

title: Manasseh repents and Amon follows wickedness
summary_text: Manasseh's long reign begins in deep evil, but after his humiliation in Babylon he humbles himself and turns again to the LORD. He removes some idolatry, yet the damage of earlier sin remains visible, and Amon quickly returns to rebellion. The chapter holds repentance and lingering consequences side by side.
key_points: Manasseh begins with severe idolatry.
He humbles himself in exile and prays.
Judah removes some idols after his return.
Amon repeats the old rebellion and dies.
evidence_phrases: did evil in the sight of the LORD

title: Josiah finds the law and begins reform
summary_text: Josiah tears down idols, repairs the temple, and then hears the book of the law read aloud. He seeks the LORD through Huldah and prepares the nation for a new covenant response. The chapter shows that reform is strongest when it starts with Scripture.
key_points: Josiah destroys idolatrous sites.
The temple is repaired.
The book of the law is found and read.
Josiah responds with humility and urgency.
evidence_phrases: seek after the God of David

title: Josiah's Passover and final death
summary_text: Josiah keeps a Passover unlike any before it, with priests, Levites, and the whole nation participating in careful order. Yet the chapter ends with his death after he ignores warning and goes out against Pharaoh Neco. The reforming king dies too soon, but the Passover remains a high point of Judah's worship.
key_points: The Passover is kept with unusual faithfulness.
Josiah receives warning from Pharaoh Neco.
He goes into battle anyway and dies.
The chapter closes a hopeful reign in sorrow.

title: Judah's last kings and the decree of Cyrus
summary_text: The final kings of Judah rebel, ignore the prophets, and lead the nation into exile as Jerusalem falls to Babylon. The temple is burned, the people are carried away, and the land rests until a new decree from Cyrus opens the door for return. The book ends in judgment but also with the first note of restoration.
key_points: The last kings reject repeated warnings.
Babylon destroys Jerusalem and the temple.
The people are carried into exile.
Cyrus issues the decree for return.
evidence_phrases: he did evil in the sight of the LORD
they mocked the messengers of God
the LORD stirred up the spirit of Cyrus
